title: The local Gan-Gross-Prasad conjecture for real unitary groups
speaker: Hang Xue
abstract: A classical branching theorem of Weyl describes how an irreducible representation of compact U(n+1)U(n+1) decomposes when restricted to U(n)U(n). The local Gan-Gross-Prasad conjecture provides a conjectural extension to the setting of representations of noncompact unitary groups lying in a generic L-packet. We prove this conjecture. Previously Beuzart-Plessis proved the ''multiplicity one in a Vogan packet'' part of the conjecture for tempered L-packets using the local trace formula approach initiated by Waldspurger.

proof_stages.label: Compact branching as motivating template
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Weyl’s compact U(n+1) to U(n) branching theorem, where highest-weight interlacing gives a multiplicity-free restriction, thereby setting the template for a noncompact branching question.

proof_stages.label: Langlands–Vogan parameters and generic packets
proof_stages.summary: For real unitary groups, irreducible representations are organized by Langlands parameters made from characters of C^×, component groups, and Vogan packets internally parametrized by characters of those component groups, with the theorem restricted to generic packets.

proof_stages.label: Multiplicity problem and root-number theorem
proof_stages.summary: The relevant smooth Fréchet branching multiplicity is defined, known to be at most one, and the local Gan-Gross-Prasad theorem is stated: in the relevant half of a product of Vogan packets there is a unique multiplicity-one pair, specified by explicit archimedean local root numbers, with a Bessel-model extension reduced to codimension one.

proof_stages.label: Proof strategy versus trace formulas
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing prior discrete-series and trace-formula work, the speaker outlines a two-step proof—first tempered packets, then reduction to tempered packets—using theta lifts and Schwartz analysis rather than local trace formulas, in a way special to real unitary groups.

proof_stages.label: Theta-lift construction up to determinant twist
proof_stages.summary: Theta correspondence supplies representations on adjacent real unitary groups whenever the parameter contains a correct-sign conjugate-self-dual character, and the key simplification is that determinant twisting preserves the relevant branching multiplicity.

proof_stages.label: Seesaw equality and reduction of packet complexity
proof_stages.summary: A two-step seesaw argument equates the original multiplicity with a multiplicity for smaller unitary groups, and more importantly lowers by two the number of correct-sign conjugate-self-dual characters in the L-parameter.

proof_stages.label: Tempered induction on a+b
proof_stages.summary: For tempered packets the theorem is proved by induction on the odd integer a+b: the base case a+b=1 is elementary, the case a≥2 follows from the seesaw reduction, and the case b≥2 follows after a parabolic-induction invariance of multiplicity.

proof_stages.label: Generic reduction by Schwartz induction
proof_stages.summary: For generic packets, irreducible parabolic inductions are handled using Schwartz induction, Borel-type tools, and Schwartz homology to replace nonunitary inducing characters one by one with unitary ones while preserving multiplicity, reducing the theorem to the tempered case.

examples.name: Weyl interlacing for compact unitary branching
examples.note: The compact restriction U(n+1) to U(n), with highest weights satisfying the sandwich/interlacing condition, illustrates the branching problem that local Gan-Gross-Prasad generalizes.
examples.engagement_reason: He sets up compact unitary branching and states Weyl’s highest-weight interlacing condition, but does not derive or compute it in a concrete instance.

passes.reason: standard compact U(n+1)↓U(n) branching family used as the motivating baseline.

Used as the compact/definite template for the theory, an endpoint signature case rather than a refutation.
Used as the positive compact prototype, with multiplicity-free interlacing giving the minimal possible branching multiplicities.

used as the non-adversarial compact benchmark; the definite compact branching case is a multiplicity-free boundary case with Weyl interlacing

examples.name: Redundant Vogan packet for a principal series
examples.note: A principal series representation viewed on U(p+1,p) and on U(p,p+1) shows concretely why a Vogan packet may contain the same abstract representation attached to different Hermitian spaces.
examples.engagement_reason: He gives the principal-series Vogan packet as a two-element redundancy example, merely identifying the two realizations on different signatures.

passes.reason: the packet collapses two formal Vogan elements to the same underlying principal-series representation, showing naive nonredundancy fails.
the packet has a collapsed redundancy, with the same principal-series representation appearing for two signatures, showing naive distinctness of Vogan-packet elements can fail
the packet collapses two signature-realizations of the same principal series and is used to show naive nonredundancy of Vogan packets fails
the Vogan packet collapses/repeats the same principal-series representation across the two signatures and is used to rule out a naive full-packet uniqueness formulation
The Vogan packet has two formal entries collapsing to the same abstract principal-series representation, illustrating why naive full-packet uniqueness fails.
The principal-series Vogan packet has a redundancy where two packet entries collapse to the same abstract representation, illustrating why a naive uniqueness statement must be qualified.
the principal-series Vogan packet shows the full packet can contain duplicated underlying representations, defeating a naive nonredundant-packet statement
The principal-series Vogan packet is used to show naive whole-packet uniqueness fails because two formal packet elements represent the same abstract representation.
The same principal-series representation occurs in two signatures, showing naive nonredundancy of Vogan packets fails.
The principal-series Vogan packet is used to show a naive packet statement needs correction, and its two entries collapse to the same underlying representation.
used to show the naive distinct-element Vogan-packet picture fails; the packet collapses to the same underlying principal-series representation twice
the principal-series Vogan packet has a collapsed duplicate of the same representation for two signatures, showing naïve packet distinctness fails

examples.name: Non-unique Whittaker model for Harish-Chandra modules
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker mentions generic Harish-Chandra modules whose Whittaker model is not unique, illustrating why the theorem is formulated for smooth Fréchet/globalized representations instead.
examples.engagement_reason: He only cites non-uniqueness of Whittaker models for Harish-Chandra modules as a warning about the category.

passes.reason: generic Harish-Chandra modules are invoked because their Whittaker models are not unique, so the desired multiplicity-one statement fails in that category.

It is explicitly cited to show that multiplicity-one/uniqueness can fail in the Harish-Chandra module category.

examples.name: Orthogonal-group L-parameters with two-dimensional summands
examples.note: In Q&A, discrete-series parameters for groups such as SO(2n+1), built from two-dimensional representations rather than characters, illustrate why the theta-lift method used here does not directly extend from real unitary groups to orthogonal groups.
examples.engagement_reason: He mentions orthogonal-group parameters with two-dimensional summands to explain why his theta-lift method does not directly apply, without working through such a parameter.

passes.reason: ordinary orthogonal-group parameters with two-dimensional summands are used as an obstruction to directly adapting the theta-lift argument from unitary groups.
